content.boundary: The demographic decline itself and its mechanical effect on cohort sizes are very strongly evidenced by KSH data; what remains uncertain is the distributional and equity consequence of passive contraction, which is only moderately or weakly evidenced.
content.change_lever: Annual births fell from ~125,000 (early 1990s) to 77,500 (2024) and ~72,000 (2025 estimate), the lowest recorded level since 1949, mechanically shrinking the pool of children available to enter any secondary track, selective or not.; A measurable demographic cliff one cohort ahead (approximately 17,000 fewer one-year-olds than four-year-olds, equivalent to 600-800 kindergarten/class-groups) means at least one hundred small schools' worth of children will structurally vanish from the system within a few years, independent of any policy choice.
content.name: Demographically-driven passive contraction (status-quo baseline)
content.system_problem: Serve as the no-new-policy counterfactual baseline against which S1-S4 are measured, in which shrinking birth cohorts force school-network consolidation and a natural decline in the selective track's absolute size and share regardless of any active equity or structural intervention.
